content.text: The Deny effect is the most direct of Azure Policy's effects: it causes the ARM request to fail with HTTP 403 (RequestDisallowedByPolicy) before the resource is created or updated. The resource never reaches the persistence layer — there is no resource to remediate afterward. Deny is enforced synchronously in the ARM request pipeline, which makes it the appropriate effect for hard compliance requirements where non-compliant resources must never exist in the environment.

attrs.alt: Deny effect ARM request evaluation flow diagram

content.text: A deny policy evaluates on every resource creation (PUT) and resource update (PATCH/PUT to an existing resource). It does not evaluate on read operations (GET) or delete operations. A resource that already exists when a deny policy is assigned is not deleted — it becomes non-compliant and appears in the compliance report, but continues running. Future updates to that existing resource are blocked if the update would leave the resource in a non-compliant configuration.
Yes, if condition matches after update
403 — update rejected; resource stays in current state
No — deny only evaluates write operations
Read succeeds regardless of compliance state
No immediate effect — no write occurred
Resource is non-compliant but continues running
What-if output shows policy denial without creating resources
Deny storage accounts with public blob access
Deny NSG rules that allow inbound RDP from internet
Parameterizing the Effect for Gradual Rollout

Hard-coding 'deny' in the policy definition prevents using the same definition in DoNotEnforce mode for testing. The recommended pattern is to expose the effect as a parameter with a default of 'deny' — this allows the same definition to be assigned with 'Audit' or 'Disabled' effect during testing, then switched to 'deny' for enforcement without replacing the definition or assignment.

# Step 1: Assign with Audit effect to see what would be denied
az policy assignment create \
  --name deny-public-storage-audit \
  --subscription $SUB_ID \
  --policy $DEF_ID \
  --params '{"effect":{"value":"audit"}}'

# Step 2: Review non-compliant resources (would-be denied)
az policy state list \
  --subscription $SUB_ID \
  --filter "policyAssignmentName eq 'deny-public-storage-audit' and complianceState eq 'NonCompliant'" \
  --query '[].resourceId' -o tsv

# Step 3: Remediate existing non-compliant resources before switching to deny
# (Updating the storage account property, or applying an exemption)

# Step 4: Switch to deny effect on the same assignment
az policy assignment update \
  --name deny-public-storage-audit \
  --subscription $SUB_ID \
  --params '{"effect":{"value":"deny"}}'

# Or use DoNotEnforce mode at the assignment level for a different approach:
az policy assignment create \
  --name deny-public-storage \
  --subscription $SUB_ID \
  --policy $DEF_ID \
  --enforcement-mode DoNotEnforce  # evaluates but does not block

Since API version 2020-09-01, policy assignments support a non-compliance message — a custom string returned in the 403 error body when the deny effect triggers. This eliminates the confusing generic error message and directs developers to the correct remediation path immediately.

# Assign a deny policy with a custom non-compliance message
az policy assignment create \
  --name deny-public-storage \
  --subscription $SUB_ID \
  --policy $DEF_ID \
  --non-compliance-message 'Storage accounts must not allow public blob access. Set allowBlobPublicAccess=false. See: https://wiki.contoso.com/azure-storage-guidelines' \
  --enforcement-mode Default

# The developer creating a storage account with public access sees:
# (RequestDisallowedByPolicy) Resource 'sa-my-app' was disallowed by policy.
# Policy identifiers: 'deny-public-storage'.
# Additional information: Storage accounts must not allow public blob access.
# Set allowBlobPublicAccess=false. See: https://wiki.contoso.com/azure-storage-guidelines

# For initiative assignments, set message per-policy-definition within the initiative:
az policy assignment create \
  --name corp-security-baseline \
  --subscription $SUB_ID \
  --policy-set-definition $INITIATIVE_ID \
  --non-compliance-message 'Corp security baseline violated. See https://wiki.contoso.com/security-baseline'

Custom non-compliance messages are one of the highest-value low-effort governance improvements. Without them, developers get cryptic 403 errors and open IT tickets. With them, the error message becomes self-service documentation that routes developers to the exact fix without human escalation.

# A developer gets 403 RequestDisallowedByPolicy during terraform apply.
# Debug workflow:

# 1. Identify which assignment caused the deny
#    The error message includes the assignment name. If using Terraform:
#    Error: creating Storage Account: (Name "sa-my-app" / Resource Group "rg-app"):
#    Code=RequestDisallowedByPolicy Message=Resource 'sa-my-app' was disallowed by policy.
#    PolicyDefinitionIds: /providers/.../policyDefinitions/deny-public-storage-accounts

# 2. Check the policy definition to understand the condition
az policy definition show \
  --name deny-public-storage-accounts \
  --management-group mg-corp \
  --query 'policyRule.if' -o json

# 3. Check if an exemption exists for this resource
az policy exemption list \
  --scope /subscriptions/$SUB_ID/resourceGroups/rg-app/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/sa-my-app \
  --query '[].{Name:name,Category:exemptionCategory,Expires:expiresOn}' -o table

# 4. Confirm the resource property causing the denial
# The deny triggered because allowBlobPublicAccess was set to true.
# Fix: set it to false in the Terraform resource:
#   allow_blob_public_access = false

# 5. Verify the fix will work before re-applying using what-if:
az deployment group what-if \
  --resource-group rg-app \
  --template-file main.bicep \
  --parameters @params.json

# 6. After fix: confirm the resource is compliant
az policy state list \
  --resource /subscriptions/$SUB_ID/resourceGroups/rg-app/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/sa-my-app \
  --query '[].{Assignment:policyAssignmentName,State:complianceState}' -o table
Bicep and ARM Template Compatibility with Deny Policies

Deny policies evaluate the resulting resource configuration regardless of whether the deployment uses the Azure portal, Azure CLI, Terraform, Bicep, or ARM templates. When a Bicep or ARM template deployment is blocked by a deny policy, the entire deployment fails — not just the individual resource. ARM template what-if lets you detect policy blocks before committing a deployment.

# ARM template what-if — detects deny policy violations before deployment
az deployment group what-if \
  --resource-group rg-prod \
  --template-file storage.bicep \
  --parameters storageAccountName=sa-myapp \
  --result-format FullResourcePayloads

# What-if output when policy would deny:
# Resource and type: Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/sa-myapp
# OperationType: Create
# After this deployment, the following policy would deny the resource:
#   Policy: deny-public-storage-accounts
#   Assignment: /subscriptions/.../policyAssignments/deny-public-storage

# Bicep build-validate to catch syntax issues before what-if:
az bicep build --file storage.bicep

# Full pipeline: build -> what-if -> deploy (only if what-if shows no blocks)
az bicep build --file storage.bicep && \
az deployment group what-if \
  --resource-group rg-prod \
  --template-file storage.json \
  --parameters storageAccountName=sa-myapp && \
az deployment group create \
  --resource-group rg-prod \
  --template-file storage.json \
  --parameters storageAccountName=sa-myapp
Deny Key Vaults without soft delete

The 'notIn' operator combined with a parameters array for the allowed list is the most reusable deny pattern — one policy definition covers any 'must use one of these values' scenario (regions, VM SKUs, TLS versions, etc.) by changing the assignment parameters rather than creating a new policy definition.

The Deny effect blocks ARM requests synchronously — the resource is never created or updated when the condition matches. Deny applies on write operations (create and update) but not reads or deletes; existing non-compliant resources continue running but become non-compliant in reporting and cannot be updated without first becoming compliant. The safe rollout pattern is to deploy the definition with an effect parameter, assign with Audit or DoNotEnforce first to identify would-be violations, remediate existing resources, then switch to deny. Custom non-compliance messages on the assignment convert cryptic 403 errors into actionable guidance. In Terraform, the enforce field maps to enforcement mode (true=Default/deny, false=DoNotEnforce) and not_scopes and non_compliance_message are first-class fields on the assignment resource.
